turns.content: I'm struggling with a task where I need to use custom structs as keys in a HashMap. My struct has a Display trait implementation and some helper methods, but I keep getting errors saying the key is not Send or Sync. I tried deriving all traits for my User struct including Hash and Eq, but that doesn't seem right since those usually require manual impl blocks.

The error you're hitting comes from trying to derive Hash and Eq on a struct that contains non-deriveable fields like Box or Option<&str>. Even if your User struct looks simple, the compiler checks every field inside it. If any field cannot be hashed or derived into PartialEq/Eq, the whole derivation fails. The core issue is understanding that deriving these traits only works when all underlying types are also deriveable and safe to hash. You cannot simply assume that because you have a Display implementation or helper methods that Hash will work automatically.

I see now about the fields inside my struct. But here's my confusion - I thought Eq implies PartialEq, so why do I need both? And if I implement Eq manually, does that automatically satisfy the PartialEq requirement too? Shouldn't just one of them be enough to use as a HashMap key?

You've hit on a critical contract detail here. In Rust's trait system, Eq actually implies PartialEq by definition - when you derive or implement Eq for a type, it also provides an implementation of PartialEq with the same logic. The PartialEq trait is just a re-exported interface that Eq uses internally. So if you successfully implement Eq correctly, you don't need to separately implement PartialEq; the compiler will automatically provide it based on your Eq implementation. However, you still cannot use such a type as a HashMap key unless you also explicitly derive or manually implement Hash, because HashMap requires both traits independently.

That clears up my confusion about Eq and PartialEq. But what if I only want partial ordering instead of total ordering? Can I skip implementing Ord entirely? And does skipping Ord affect whether I can use the type as a HashMap key?

You absolutely do not need to implement Ord for your struct to be usable as a HashMap key. Ord defines a total order relationship, which is much stricter than what you typically need for map keys. Hash and Eq are sufficient for most use cases involving collections like HashMap or HashSet where elements act as unique identifiers rather than sorted values. If you only care about partial ordering - meaning some pairs might not be comparable - you can safely omit Ord entirely without breaking your ability to store items in a HashMap, since those traits don't depend on each other.

Wait, I thought the derived macros would automatically give me all four traits? Why did my derivation fail then if I tried deriving Hash and Eq together? Is there something wrong with how the derive macro handles multiple traits at once?

The derive macro doesn't inherently conflict when combining Hash and Eq - it's about what types your struct actually contains. If you try to derive both on a struct that has fields like Option<String> or Box<i32>, those specific field types might not support hashing in the way the compiler expects, causing the entire derivation block to fail. The derive macro checks each field recursively; if any single field cannot be hashed or derived into PartialEq/Eq, the whole operation fails regardless of how many traits you're requesting together. You need to ensure every constituent type supports all the required traits before attempting to derive them.

Ah okay, so I should probably implement Hash and Eq manually instead? But that seems tedious - what's the benefit beyond avoiding compilation errors? Is there a performance difference between manual implementations versus derived ones for HashMap lookups?

Manual implementations of Hash and Eq give you full control over how values are compared and hashed, which can be crucial when dealing with complex data structures or specialized algorithms. While basic types like i32 or String often perform identically whether using derive or manual impl blocks, custom logic becomes necessary when your struct contains non-deriveable components or when you need to implement special comparison rules that don't fit standard Rust patterns. In most practical scenarios involving HashMap lookups, the performance difference between derived and manual implementations is negligible for simple types.

One last thing - I noticed someone mentioned that using Vec as a key in a HashMap requires implementing Ord too. Why does that happen specifically? And what happens if my vector contains non-deriveable items like custom structs?

Vec implements Ord by default because it needs to support sorting operations and other ordered collections where total ordering is essential for stability. When you try to use a Vec as a HashMap key, the compiler checks whether that type can be compared against itself in a consistent, total order - which is exactly what Ord provides. If your vector contains custom structs that don't implement PartialEq or Eq properly, then even though Vec itself has Ord, those internal elements break the chain because the comparison logic inside Vec relies on comparing its contained items using those traits.
I'm looking at that new struct you wrote for the user profile service. It feels a lot like a HashMap key to me since I need to look up users by ID quickly in our main collection.

You're right, it acts as a key because we use Hash and Eq traits so the standard library can put it into a HashMap without collisions. The implementation you showed uses derive macros to generate all four traits at once, which saves typing.
Wait, I thought if I only needed equality checks then deriving Eq was enough and Hash should be optional? Why did you add Ord too?

You're partially right that HashMap doesn't strictly require Ord for insertion, but our sorting utility later in the codebase needs total ordering. The contract says Eq implies PartialEq automatically, so those two go together as a pair, but Hash and Ord are separate requirements depending on your use case.
So if I just need to store unique items without sorting then I can skip the manual implementation for Ord? But what happens if my struct changes and breaks that assumption?

Exactly, you only implement the traits your code actually needs. If you remove the sort function but keep the HashMap usage, then dropping Ord is fine since it's not part of the Eq or Hash contracts. The derive macro generates manual implementations if you don't provide them explicitly.
That makes sense about the partial order thing but I'm worried about the HashMap key requirement. Can I use a struct with only PartialEq and Hash as keys?

Yes, you absolutely can use structs with just Eq and Hash as keys in a HashMap since those are the minimum requirements for collision resolution. Ord is only needed when sorting or using binary search trees like BTreeMap.
Okay so I should remove that manual Ord implementation from my struct and just rely on derive to get everything else done?

You can drop the manual Ord if you don't need it, but keep Hash and Eq since those are required for your HashMap usage. The derive macro will still generate all four traits even if you only use two of them in practice.
I'm trying to use my User struct as a key in a HashMap but it's failing with an error about missing the Hash trait. I've tried adding derive! and it still won't compile.

That is a common trap for beginners. The derive macro only generates implementations if you specify which traits you want, so if you ran #[derive(Debug)] alone, Hash was never generated. You must explicitly add #[derive(PartialEq, Eq, Hash)] to those lines because the compiler needs concrete code to implement that trait.
Okay I added all three and it compiles now but then I tried to use a struct with custom fields like timestamps or calculated scores as keys. Why does that fail when I just add the derive macro?

The issue is that Hash requires a total ordering of values for consistency, but your custom logic might not guarantee that every two different instances produce distinct hashes or that equal inputs always yield equal outputs. While PartialEq ensures logical equality, the HashMap contract demands that Hash and Eq be perfectly synchronized so identical keys never collide.
I see now I need to implement those manually but I'm worried about breaking logic if I get the order wrong. How do I know what fields actually matter for identity?

Think of it like a physical key on a door; only certain parts of your structure define who you are, not all of it. You should implement Hash and Eq by including the exact fields that uniquely identify an object in your domain, ignoring metadata like timestamps or computed scores.
That makes sense so I only hash the id field? But what if I need to sort my users later and use Ord?

If you plan to sort your collection, you will also need to implement Ord which requires a total order on all comparable fields. However, remember that Ord implies both Eq and Hash so once you write the comparison logic correctly for Ord, deriving or implementing those other traits becomes straightforward.
I'm trying to make a HashMap key but I thought if I just derive the traits it would work fine. I derived Hash and Eq so I assumed that was enough to put my struct in a HashMap without any issues.

You've made a very common mistake here. While deriving Hash and PartialEq is necessary, you also need Eq because HashMap requires equality checks for duplicate keys. Actually, the standard library documentation states that Eq implies PartialEq, so your struct must implement both traits to be used as a valid key in a HashMap.
But I saw examples where people only derive one of them and it still worked. Is there a difference between deriving manually versus using the macro?

The macro automatically generates code that satisfies all three traits: Eq, PartialEq, and Hash for your struct. When you use the derive macro, Rust ensures these contracts are met consistently without errors. If you implement them manually, you risk breaking those relationships because manual implementations often forget to satisfy the contract requirements.
I understand now that I need all three traits but I'm confused about Ord. Does implementing Hash mean my data can be sorted?

Not necessarily. Implementing Hash only tells Rust how to calculate a hash value for your type, which is used by HashMap to find buckets. It does not guarantee an ordering relationship between values so your data cannot be sorted just because you have Hash implemented.
So I can use my struct as a HashMap key but not sort it? That seems like a major limitation in my design.

That is exactly how Rust traits are designed to separate concerns. You only need Ord if you want to sort or compare your data, while Hash is specifically for map lookups. These are independent contracts so they don't interfere with each other.
Okay so I can keep using the derive macro and just add Ord if I ever need sorting later? That makes sense now.

Exactly! The derive macro provides all necessary traits for your struct, so you can use it as a HashMap key immediately. If you later decide to sort data, you simply add Ord to the derive list in your definition and everything will work seamlessly.
